name: Mutation Intent
summary: Protocol owning the write-lifecycle axis (apply → reconcile → rollback) — symmetric to the Loader Intent for reads (#1395 Fork 1a). Models how a write UX behaves: optimistic vs pessimistic apply, how a success reconciles against the source of truth, how a failed optimistic apply rolls back, double-submit prevention, and trigger busy-state. The running implementation already ships as the resource-action block (WE's useMutation); this intent names the axis that block was squatting on loader.strategy for. Default strategy = pessimistic (always-safe; optimism is the opt-in). Composes loader (read/write split), reliability (the failure branch), action (trigger visual-weight), and the change-record reversibility primitive (shared with undo/redo #1394).

strategy.description: When the predicted result is applied relative to the server round-trip. `pessimistic` (default) awaits the server, then applies — the simplest always-correct behavior. `optimistic` applies a predicted result immediately and reconciles/rolls back on settle — the opt-in enhancement (it requires a predicted result the author supplies). Both are legitimate end-states (a real dimension, not a baked mechanic); the universal read/write split (TanStack useQuery/useMutation, SWR, Apollo) is mirrored here.

reconcile.description: How a successful write settles the predicted/local state against the authoritative server response. `invalidate` (default) marks dependent reads stale and refetches (TanStack invalidateQueries). `merge` applies the server's authoritative response into the cache in place (Apollo cache update / SWR populateCache). `trust` keeps the optimistic value with no settle (only safe when the server echoes nothing meaningful). Reconcile is meaningful for both strategies — a pessimistic write still chooses how dependent reads refresh.

rollback.description: What a failed write restores. `snapshot` (default) restores the pre-apply state from the change-record `oldValue` snapshot taken at dispatch (RFC 6902 ops are not self-inverting, so the snapshot is carried). Under `optimistic` a rollback path is a FIXED mechanic — an optimistic apply with no revert is broken — so `none` is only valid under `pessimistic`, where nothing was applied locally and there is nothing to undo. Shares its reversibility record with undo/redo (#1394) via the Change Tracking protocol.

doubleSubmit.description: How concurrent dispatches of the same write are guarded — a FIXED-mechanic concern every incumbent handles. `prevent` (default) disables the trigger while a write is in flight (the resource-action busy-state). `queue` serializes repeated dispatches in order. `allow` permits concurrent in-flight writes (only for genuinely independent, idempotent operations).

busy.description: What the user experiences on the triggering control while the write is pending — composes the Action Intent's `busy` axis. `disabled` (default) makes the trigger non-interactive during the write (reinforces `doubleSubmit: prevent`); `interactive` keeps it operable (e.g. to cancel). Visual prominence of the trigger itself stays owned by `action` (level/tone); this dimension is only the pending experience.

description:
What This Is

The write-lifecycle intent — the apply→reconcile→rollback axis, symmetric to the Loader Intent for reads (placement ratified in #1395, Fork 1a). The running lifecycle already ships as the resource-action block — WE's useMutation — but it was declaring implementsIntent: action (pure visual-weight) and wiring its optimistic trait to loader.strategy.optimistic (a read/blocking dimension). This intent names the write-side axis that was missing, so the mutation lifecycle stops squatting on the loader's.

Strategy

When the predicted result is applied relative to the server round-trip. The read/write split is near-universal (TanStack useQuery/useMutation, SWR, Apollo).

  • pessimistic: Default. Await the server, then apply. The simplest always-correct behavior.
  • optimistic: Apply a predicted result immediately, reconcile/roll back on settle. The opt-in enhancement — it requires a predicted result the author supplies.

Reconcile

How a successful write settles local/predicted state against the authoritative server response.

  • invalidate: Default. Mark dependent reads stale and refetch (TanStack invalidateQueries).
  • merge: Apply the server's authoritative response into the cache in place (Apollo cache update, SWR populateCache).
  • trust: Keep the value with no settle (only when the server echoes nothing meaningful).

Rollback (fixed under optimistic)

What a failed write restores. An optimistic apply with no revert path is broken, so rollback is a fixed mechanic under optimistic.

  • snapshot: Default. Restore the pre-apply state from the change-record oldValue snapshot taken at dispatch (RFC 6902 ops are not self-inverting, so the snapshot is carried). This is the same reversibility record undo/redo (#1394) applies its inverse-ops from — both route through the Change Tracking protocol.
  • none: Only valid under pessimistic — nothing was applied locally, so there is nothing to undo.

Double-Submit (fixed mechanic)

Guarding concurrent dispatches of the same write — every incumbent handles it.

  • prevent: Default. Disable the trigger while a write is in flight.
  • queue: Serialize repeated dispatches in order.
  • allow: Permit concurrent in-flight writes (only for independent, idempotent operations).

Busy (composes Action)

The pending experience on the triggering control — composes the Action Intent's busy axis. disabled (default) reinforces doubleSubmit: prevent; interactive keeps the trigger operable (e.g. to cancel). The trigger's visual prominence (level/tone) stays owned by action.

Composition (all supported seams, no forks)

  • ↔ loader: the read/write split — loader owns the pending/blocking UX for reads, mutation owns the write lifecycle.
  • ↔ reliability: the failure branch — retry policy, timeout, and error tolerance on a failed write live there; mutation hands off once the write times out or fails.
  • ↔ action: the trigger's visual weight + busy state.
  • ↔ Change Tracking / change-record: the reversibility record snapshot rollback and undo/redo both consume.

Interface Protocol

type MutationStrategy = 'pessimistic' | 'optimistic';
type MutationReconcile = 'invalidate' | 'merge' | 'trust';
type MutationRollback = 'snapshot' | 'none';
type MutationDoubleSubmit = 'prevent' | 'queue' | 'allow';
type MutationBusy = 'disabled' | 'interactive';

interface MutationIntent {
    // When the predicted result is applied relative to the round-trip
    strategy: MutationStrategy;        // default 'pessimistic'
    // How a success settles against the source of truth
    reconcile?: MutationReconcile;     // default 'invalidate'
    // What a failed write restores (fixed 'snapshot' under optimistic)
    rollback?: MutationRollback;       // default 'snapshot'
    // Concurrent-dispatch guard
    doubleSubmit?: MutationDoubleSubmit; // default 'prevent'
    // Pending experience on the trigger (composes action.busy)
    busy?: MutationBusy;               // default 'disabled'
}

Running implementation: resource-action (POST/PUT/DELETE, withOptimisticMutation, the resource-action-revert rollback event). Grounded in the placement survey (optimistic-mutation lifecycle).
